title: Turraea humilis
intro: Turraea humilis, commonly known as the "Turraea plant" or "African dog tooth," is a medicinal plant species native to Africa, belonging to the family Meliaceae. The plant is valued for its traditional use in treating various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues, due to its purported health benefits. Bioactive constituents of Turraea humilis include lignans, flavonoids, and alkaloids, which have been reported to possess antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties. Traditionally, the plant is prepared as a tea, decoction, or infusion, and also used in combination with other herbs in various herbal preparations, including capsules and extracts.
taxonomy: Turraea humilis is classified within the family Simaroubaceae, a family of flowering plants commonly known as the family of the 'quassia' or 'monkey tree' trees. The species belongs to the genus Turraea, which consists of several species of flowering plants native to tropical Africa. Turraea humilis is a shrub or small tree that is native to tropical Africa, particularly in the regions of central and western Africa. The classification of Turraea humilis is further supported by its characteristic flowers and fruits, which are typical of the Simaroubaceae family. Overall, the classification of Turraea humilis is consistent with the family and genus classification of its closest relatives within the Simaroubaceae family.

It is often employed in the treatment of various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin infections. The roots and stems of Turraea humilis are commonly used in herbal remedies, showcasing the plant's versatility in traditional medicine. Further research is necessary to confirm the efficacy and safety of Turraea humilis in modern medicine.
